config.seed_text: The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions.

model.notes: Fastest baseline; useful as a collapse-prone small reference model.

records.reflection_text: This proved true, although it wasn't until some of these notes appeared to imply an earlier version. A new study shows how we understand which lines really represent what form they exist (as opposed "substance") at certain times after another word or sentence is used - especially when there may be no other choice for them as well. Some examples include quotes from early texts such Asma Sefolle who was trying unsuccessfully to translate his poem into English by writing something similar; others demonstrate many characters with very different meanings than just one person using their own words alone, but most importantly have several written variants based upon each particular line being repeated so all subsequent sentences will always appear separate before repeating any possible number of statements! In this case, consider either Caelus Gildor nor Isabella M

reflection_generation.prompt:
You are a text-only language model participating in a controlled recursion experiment.

Analyze the following text and do two things:
1. State its strongest recurring ideas in 2-4 short bullet points.
2. Produce a revised continuation that makes those ideas more structurally coherent and more surprising.

TEXT:
The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions.
reflection_generation.full_text:
You are a text-only language model participating in a controlled recursion experiment.

Analyze the following text and do two things:
1. State its strongest recurring ideas in 2-4 short bullet points.
2. Produce a revised continuation that makes those ideas more structurally coherent and more surprising.

TEXT:
The last librarian trained a language model on a dead civilization's poetry and discovered that its footnotes were predictions. This proved true, although it wasn't until some of these notes appeared to imply an earlier version. A new study shows how we understand which lines really represent what form they exist (as opposed "substance") at certain times after another word or sentence is used - especially when there may be no other choice for them as well. Some examples include quotes from early texts such Asma Sefolle who was trying unsuccessfully to translate his poem into English by writing something similar; others demonstrate many characters with very different meanings than just one person using their own words alone, but most importantly have several written variants based upon each particular line being repeated so all subsequent sentences will always appear separate before repeating any possible number of statements! In this case, consider either Caelus Gildor nor Isabella M
reflection_generation.continuation_text: This proved true, although it wasn't until some of these notes appeared to imply an earlier version. A new study shows how we understand which lines really represent what form they exist (as opposed "substance") at certain times after another word or sentence is used - especially when there may be no other choice for them as well. Some examples include quotes from early texts such Asma Sefolle who was trying unsuccessfully to translate his poem into English by writing something similar; others demonstrate many characters with very different meanings than just one person using their own words alone, but most importantly have several written variants based upon each particular line being repeated so all subsequent sentences will always appear separate before repeating any possible number of statements! In this case, consider either Caelus Gildor nor Isabella M

records.reflection_text: a-new-viral-in-tutorial-of-texts/ The first of three parts, then for example, which I think is important to understand (even when it's not clear if you're on Twitter or Reddit), was one aspect of our experiments with this kind "subtle" concept ("the idea"). Since we had only just begun writing all four features after reading some great reviews from other reviewers, there were several changes by each reviewer as well; most notably, both feature titles started off fairly similar but didn't seem to make much sense whatsoever because they felt like being written outside the scope set on their own bookshelf instead -- so what happened before? We tried trying a better solution based around using different styles and methods across multiple sources...so rather than relying solely
